title_en: Introduction to Tuhfat al-Muhtaj by Ibn Hajar al-Haytami
title_ar: مقدمة في تحفة المحتاج لابن حجر الهيتمي
content_en:
Tuhfat al-Muhtaj fi Sharh al-Minhaj ('The Gift for the Seeker in Explaining the Minhaj') is one of the three supreme reference works of late Shafi'i scholarship, a detailed commentary on an-Nawawi's Minhaj at-Talibin authored by Shihab ad-Din Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn 'Ali ibn Hajar al-Haytami al-Makki (909–974 AH / 1504–1567 CE). Together with Mughni al-Muhtaj by al-Khatib ash-Sharbini and Nihayat al-Muhtaj by ar-Ramli, Tuhfat al-Muhtaj forms the highest tier of Shafi'i legal reference, consulted whenever the Minhaj's conciseness leaves room for dispute.

Ibn Hajar al-Haytami should not be confused with his earlier namesake, the hadith master Ibn Hajar al-'Asqalani (d. 852 AH). The later Ibn Hajar — the jurist — was Egyptian-born but spent much of his scholarly career in Mecca, which is why he is often called 'al-Makki.' He became the leading Shafi'i authority of the Hijaz in his era and was frequently consulted by Muslim communities across the Indian Ocean world.

Ibn Hajar al-Haytami is also known for his collections of fatwas (Al-Fatawa al-Kubra al-Fiqhiyyah) and his important theological works, including works refuting Sufi practices he considered deviant and defending positions within the Ash'ari-Shafi'i tradition. He is sometimes criticized by later scholars for his occasionally strict positions on certain questions, but his scholarly rigor and the breadth of his knowledge are universally acknowledged.

The method of Tuhfat al-Muhtaj is to take each phrase or ruling of the Minhaj at-Talibin and expand it with: the evidential basis, the reasoning, the alternative positions within the Shafi'i school, engagement with positions of other schools where relevant, and — importantly — the notes (hawashi) of later scholars that have been incorporated into the text over centuries. The printed editions of Tuhfat al-Muhtaj are accompanied by the hawashi of Ibn 'Abd ash-Shafi and al-'Abbadi, making them effectively multi-layered scholarly dialogues.

In Southeast Asia — particularly Indonesia, Malaysia, and the surrounding region — Tuhfat al-Muhtaj has historically been the primary Shafi'i reference for both teaching and fatwa. The region's Islamic scholars, trained in the Hadhrami and Egyptian Shafi'i traditions, brought this text with them and built their legal tradition upon it. Its influence in East Africa follows a similar pattern.

Mastering Tuhfat al-Muhtaj is the goal of advanced Shafi'i legal education. The scholar who can navigate this text fluently — understanding its references, engaging its arguments, and applying its positions — has reached the highest level of classical Shafi'i fiqh scholarship.
